bibliographic.title: The Lay Folks’ Catechism; or, The English and Latin Versions of Archbishop Thoresby’s Instruction for the People; Together with a Wycliffite Adaptation of the Same, and the Corresponding Canons of the Council of Lambeth

entities.description: EETS parallel-text edition coordinating the English and Latin Thoresby texts, the Lambeth catechetical compilation with York additions, and Peckham’s Lambeth canons. Simmons prepared the texts, notes, and draft reference matter; Nolloth completed and revised the volume and wrote its introduction.
entities.label: Simmons and Nolloth, The Lay Folks’ Catechism (1901)

physicalDescription.extent: xxxix preliminary pages and 137 Arabic-numbered pages
notes: CME00041 represents the title matter and printed pp. 1–99; the cached scan retains the complete volume, including the introduction and pp. 101–137 of notes, glossary, and index.

rights.basis: Published in 1901; Internet Archive metadata marks the scanned volume NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT and reports no visible copyright notice.

access.entityLabel: Borthwick Institute for Archives, Abp Reg 11
access.format: Official register description and folio browser

entities.description: Archiepiscopal register containing the English instruction at ff. 295r–297r and the Latin instruction at ff. 297v–298v. Modern scholarship identifies Thomas de Aldefeld as scribe and describes the leaves as an independent quaternion, possibly an official exemplar before binding into the register.
entities.label: Borthwick Institute for Archives, Abp Reg 11

physicalDescription.extent: 400 numbered folios plus binding matter and flyleaves exposed in the public image sequence
notes: The corpus passages occupy ff. 295r–298v, public image positions 655–662.
The complete public sequence additionally exposes boards, pastedowns, guards, flyleaves, binding fragments, extra f. 56r gutter views, and a final color-reference image.

rights.basis: The texts were composed and copied in the fourteenth century. This conclusion does not apply to modern photographs or metadata.

notes: The exact official record URL returned HTTP 403 to this research environment; shelfmark and target folios are independently supported by the edition and Greig.

rights.basis: The order form does not grant publication rights; the separate permission form limits approved use, requires acknowledgement, can charge a fee, and provides nonexclusive permission for a specific use and period.

notes: A dated search of the official LUNA image collection and indexed public records found no complete exposed MS 408 digital object. This is a negative discovery claim, not proof that no internal digitization exists.

entities.description: Composite codex whose fifteenth-century first booklet contains at ff. 1r–17r the eclectic catechetical compilation printed as text L. Modern research separates its Pater Noster, Ave Maria, Creed, Ten Commandments, and related compatible materials and rejects the 1901 theory that the compilation is a Wycliffite adaptation of Thoresby’s instruction.
entities.label: Lambeth Palace Library, MS 408

physicalDescription.extent: 129 principal physical folios with complex slipped inserts; Booklet I ff. 1r–17r contains the text represented here
physicalDescription.format: Composite codex bound in calf over wooden boards
notes: Earlier descriptions counted 141 leaves by treating foliated slips separately.
The first booklet’s language has Norfolk, Ely, and Lincolnshire associations; this is witness language, not secure evidence for the compilation’s place of composition.

rights.basis: The manuscript content is medieval; modern catalog descriptions and reproductions remain separate rights components.

bibliographic.title: The Lay Folks’ Catechism, Alliterative Verse, and Cursus

entities.description: Peer-reviewed study of the English catechism’s commission, witness tradition, form, and rhetorical rhythm. It identifies twenty-six witnesses, treats Abp Reg 11 and Bodleian Don.c.13 as the two best, and argues that the English text is a deliberately plain, category-defying form rather than confidently identifiable traditional alliterative verse.
entities.label: Ian Cornelius, ‘The Lay Folks’ Catechism, Alliterative Verse, and Cursus’ (2019)

rights.basis: The cached author manuscript states © The Author 2018 and Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 3.0.

bibliographic.citation: Pamela L. C. Greig, The ‘Lay Folks’ Catechism’—An Edition (PhD thesis, University of Nottingham, 2018).
bibliographic.title: The ‘Lay Folks’ Catechism’—An Edition

entities.description: Doctoral thesis and modern critical edition using Bodleian Don.c.13 as base and variants from twenty-six witnesses. It re-evaluates the 1901 edition, rejects the Wycliffite-adaptation theory for Lambeth MS 408, explains that York Minster XVI.L.12 is not a Lay Folks’ Catechism witness, and cautions against treating Peckham’s canons as a proven direct source.
entities.label: Pamela L. C. Greig, The ‘Lay Folks’ Catechism’—An Edition (2018)

rights.basis: The repository copy states All Rights Reserved and provides no open-content license.

reviewNotes: The graph treats the four coordinated medieval documents, physical witnesses, 1901 edition, U-M encoding, repository XML, and digital facsimiles as distinct layers.

The 1901 phrase ‘Wycliffite adaptation’ is preserved as attribution history only. Modern research describes Lambeth MS 408, ff. 1r–17r as an eclectic catechetical compilation and rejects both Wycliffite authorship and its treatment as a clean reworking of the Lay Folks’ Catechism.
Similarity between Peckham’s 1281 pastoral canons and Thoresby’s 1357 program does not establish direct textual derivation; no copied_from relation is asserted between P and T or C.

The complete Abp Reg 11 cache is complete relative to the provider’s 893 published image resources, not a claim to preservation-master completeness. The cached JPEGs are full-color 1800-pixel-wide access derivatives.

The official Abp Reg 11 Presentation 2 document places Canvas objects directly in sequences rather than inside a conventional Sequence.canvases array. The bundle therefore inventories every official image resource without silently repairing the published manifest.
A search of the existing source cache found no exact Abp Reg 11 bundle suitable for hardlink reuse before download.

lineage.summary: CME00041 is the repository XML representation of the title matter and printed pp. 1–99 of Thomas Frederick Simmons and Henry Edward Nolloth’s 1901 EETS parallel-text volume. That source coordinates four medieval documents: the Middle English instruction commissioned by Archbishop John Thoresby from John de Gaitrik (T), Thoresby’s Latin instruction (C), an eclectic Middle English catechetical compilation copied in Lambeth Palace Library MS 408 and historically but misleadingly called a Wycliffite adaptation (L), and John Peckham’s Latin Ignorancia sacerdotum canons (P). The edition transcribes T and C from Borthwick Institute Abp Reg 11, L principally from Lambeth MS 408 with bracketed additions from York Minster MS XVI.L.12, and does not identify the copy used for P. A complete 198-page scan of the 1901 volume and the complete 893-image object exposed for Abp Reg 11 are cached. The register bundle retains boards, pastedowns, guards, flyleaves, binding fragments, all exposed numbered folios, extra gutter views, and the color reference; the corpus passages are mapped within it to sequence positions 655–662, ff. 295r–298v.

catalogSummary.author: John de Gaitrik; John Thoresby; John Peckham; anonymous catechetical compiler
catalogSummary.date: 1281 and 1357 medieval components; Lambeth compilation date unresolved; edition published 1901
catalogSummary.editor: Thomas Frederick Simmons and Henry Edward Nolloth
catalogSummary.region: England, especially York/Yorkshire and Lambeth; London publication imprint
catalogSummary.title: The Lay Folks’ Catechism: 1901 parallel-text collection

catalogingSubject.label: The Lay Folks’ Catechism: 1901 parallel-text collection
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The edition-level composite represented by University of Michigan CME item CME00041: title matter and printed pp. 1–99 of Simmons and Nolloth’s 1901 EETS volume, coordinating four distinct medieval documents labeled here T, C, L, and P. This record does not collapse John de Gaitrik’s Middle English instruction, Archbishop Thoresby’s Latin instruction, the eclectic Lambeth MS 408 catechetical compilation, Peckham’s Lambeth canons, their physical witnesses, the complete printed volume, the U-M encoding, or the repository XML into one abstract work. The inherited phrase ‘Wycliffite adaptation’ is recorded as historical editorial framing, not accepted modern authorship or textual classification.

contentParts.label: T — The Lay Folks’ Catechism, Middle English instruction
notes: The XML’s 581 L elements describe the edition/encoding presentation, not a settled metrical classification.
contentParts.summary: John de Gaitrik’s 1357 clear vernacular rendering/adaptation commissioned by Archbishop John Thoresby for lay pastoral instruction. The 1901 edition presents it in verse-like lines, although the Reg 11 copy is punctuated as prose and modern scholarship does not confidently identify a traditional alliterative metre.

contentParts.label: C — Archbishop Thoresby’s Latin instruction
contentParts.summary: The Latin pastoral instruction associated with Archbishop John Thoresby and the York convocation, coordinating doctrinal topics and the teaching expected of clergy.

contentParts.label: L — Anonymous Middle English catechetical compilation
notes: Its 969 L and 54 P elements describe the XML representation, not a claim that all material originated in one mixed-form authorial composition.
contentParts.summary: An eclectic grouping of compatible catechetical texts copied in the fifteenth-century first booklet of Lambeth Palace Library MS 408, including treatments of the Pater Noster, Ave Maria, Creed, commandments, and related teaching. The edition adds bracketed material from York Minster MS XVI.L.12. Modern scholarship rejects its older description as a Wycliffite adaptation of the Lay Folks’ Catechism.

contentParts.label: P — John Peckham’s Ignorancia sacerdotum
notes: The 1901 copy source or base witness for P remains unidentified; no manuscript locator is invented.
contentParts.summary: Latin canons ix–xiii associated with John Peckham and the Council of Lambeth of 1281, printed as a pastoral comparison text. Similarity of program does not establish direct textual derivation of the Thoresby instructions.

dateStatements.display: The Middle English instruction was commissioned and produced in 1357
notes: The commission is addressed to John de Gaitrik; Thoresby’s schedule was issued at Cawood on 25 November 1357. This date is composition/translation context, not merely witness-copying date.

dateStatements.display: Thoresby’s Latin instruction was promulgated in 1357
Peckham’s Ignorancia sacerdotum canons belong to the Council of Lambeth of 1281
The represented parallel-text edition was published in 1901

extent.description: Four serialized medieval text streams, T/C/L/P. T is classified as other rather than forced into prose or verse; C and P are prose, and L is mixed. The XML contains 1,550 L in twenty-five LG, 215 P, 176 PB, 558 MILESTONE, and 413 NOTE elements. Direct-component counts are T 581 L, C 95 P, L 969 L plus 54 P, and P 57 P. The 176 PB elements represent 101 unique printed REF values because source streams reuse the edition’s parallel-page coordinates.

formStatements.description: Composite of Latin prose, Middle English prose, and verse-like or mixed lineated presentation. This whole-record form summarizes the four coordinated documents rather than assigning one form to each.

Verse-like editorial lineation but a deliberately plain, category-defying form. The authoritative Reg 11 copy is punctuated as continuous prose, other early northern witnesses use lineation, and modern analysis does not confidently identify traditional alliterative metre.
Latin pastoral instruction encoded in prose paragraphs.

The 1901 edition and XML alternate lineated and prose representations within the eclectic Middle English compilation. This is an editorial/encoding description and does not prove one original authorial form.
Latin ecclesiastical canons encoded in prose paragraphs.

genres.label: Scholarly edition
notes: Edition-level genre of the cataloging subject, not a medieval genre assigned to each constituent document.

scope.description: Modern English title matter, overview, headings, labels, and notes in the represented 1901 edition

notes: The composite unit reflects what CME00041 actually encodes. It is not a claim that T, C, L, and P form one medieval intellectual work.
The preferred spelling John de Gaitrik follows the direct commission as interpreted by modern scholarship; historical variants remain alternate names.
John Thoresby is assigned authorship of C and recorded as commissioner of T in notes; the absent commissioner role is not approximated with a misleading schema role.
John Wyclif is not added as an agent because ‘Wycliffite’ in the 1901 title does not mean Wyclif authored L, and the underlying theory is rejected.
The witness language and fifteenth-century date of Lambeth MS 408 are not silently promoted to the composition date or place of L.
Peckham’s pastoral canons are a comparison component, not an asserted textual ancestor of the Thoresby documents.
The source-lineage manifest retains complete downloaded source objects and separately maps the corpus portions within them.

next_steps: Review modern component-level source and dialect studies.
Do not transfer the witness’s date or East Midland language mechanically to the compilation’s composition.
openQuestions.question: When and where was the eclectic catechetical compilation represented by L assembled, independently of the date and dialect of Lambeth MS 408’s fifteenth-century copy?

next_steps: Trace the edition’s notes and modern Peckham editions for an explicit source.
Add a source-witness binding only when evidence identifies it.
openQuestions.question: What physical or printed source underlies the 1901 edition’s P text?

next_steps: Obtain a whole-manuscript facsimile if possible and collate it against the complete 1901 edition.
Record target folios inside the complete object without redefining the manuscript as a Lay Folks’ Catechism witness.
openQuestions.question: Which exact folios of York Minster MS XVI.L.12 supply each bracketed passage in L?

next_steps: Review subsequent responses to Cornelius and current Middle English prosody scholarship.
Keep manuscript punctuation, editorial lineation, and metrical classification as distinct claims.
openQuestions.question: What controlled form label should ultimately replace ‘other’ for T without imposing a prose/verse binary that the manuscript and metrical evidence do not support?

notes: John de Gaitrik was a monk of St Mary’s Abbey, York; the statement gives institutional and commissioning context rather than a claimed exact desk of composition.
Northern Middle English and the York administrative context support this broad region; it is kept distinct from the place where any later witness was copied.
The pastoral schedule was issued at Cawood on 25 November 1357; this administrative association is not automatically the exact place where every word was composed.
The canons correspond to the Council of Lambeth of 1281; this historical setting is distinct from the location of the unidentified copy used by the 1901 editors.
London is the publication imprint, not a medieval composition place.
Thoresby’s 1357 commission addresses Gaitrik and directs speed and clarity in rendering the instruction for lay teaching.
Translator and adapter are both retained because T is a vernacular pastoral rendering designed for clear oral use, not a mechanically word-for-word transfer.

The Latin instruction belongs to Thoresby’s archiepiscopal pastoral program and was promulgated through the York convocation. Thoresby also commissioned T; the schema has no commissioner role, so that relationship remains in notes rather than being miscast as authorship of T.
Modern research treats L as an eclectic grouping of compatible catechetical texts. It is not assigned to John Wyclif or an unspecified Wycliffite writer.
P represents canons ix–xiii associated with Peckham and the Council of Lambeth in 1281.
Simmons saw the four texts through typesetting and assembled notes and draft glossary/index material before his death.
Nolloth completed and revised the volume, completed its notes and reference matter, and wrote the introduction.

subjects.label: Christian doctrine and the Ten Commandments
Church reform, clerical authority, and religious orders

summaries.value: A scholarly parallel-text collection centered on late-medieval pastoral instruction. T is John de Gaitrik’s 1357 Middle English rendering of Archbishop John Thoresby’s program for teaching the laity the Creed, commandments, works of mercy, sacraments, virtues, and vices; C is the corresponding Latin archiepiscopal instruction. L is a separate anonymous Middle English catechetical compilation copied in Lambeth MS 408, augmented in the edition from York Minster MS XVI.L.12, and no longer responsibly described as a Wycliffite adaptation. P presents John Peckham’s 1281 Latin canons on clerical instruction as a comparison text, without a proven direct textual source relation to T or C.

Four coordinated medieval components: T, the Middle English instruction in verse-like editorial lineation; C, Thoresby’s Latin instruction; L, an eclectic Middle English catechetical compilation in mixed presentation; and P, Peckham’s Latin Ignorancia sacerdotum canons. CME00041 also includes the 1901 title matter and text overview but excludes the edition introduction, notes, glossary, and index.

The preferred title and composite unit intentionally describe the encoded 1901 arrangement. Users seeking only the abstract Lay Folks’ Catechism should follow part T and its Abp Reg 11 witness mapping rather than treating all four texts as one medieval work.

The full 1901 title is retained verbatim for historical identification, but John Wyclif is not assigned authorship and L is not classified as Wycliffite. Likewise, P is not modeled as a copied source of T or C merely because the pastoral programs overlap.

notes: The qualifier distinguishes this coordinated edition-level object from the abstract Middle English work conventionally called The Lay Folks’ Catechism.
titles.value: The Lay Folks’ Catechism: 1901 parallel-text collection

notes: ‘Wycliffite adaptation’ is a 1901 title-page claim now rejected for text L; preserving the title is not endorsing the attribution.

notes: A useful modern descriptive title rather than a securely authorial medieval title.

assertion.notes: Supported only as a historical edition label; modern scholarship rejects its Wycliffite and clean-adaptation claims.

notes: Preferred description: anonymous eclectic Middle English catechetical compilation.
